is defined in the [ISO/TC 211](/wiki/ISO/TC_211 "ISO/TC 211") series of standards as the "[discipline](/wiki/Academic_discipline "Academic discipline") concerned with the collection, distribution, storage, analysis, processing, presentation of [geographic data or geographic information](/wiki/Geographic_data_or_geographic_information "Geographic data or geographic information")".[[1]](#cite_note-1) Under another definition, it consists of products, services and tools involved in the collection, integration and management of geographic (geospatial) data.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Surveying engineering was the common name used for geomatics engineering in the past. The term was placed by the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [Encyclopedia of Life Support Systems](/wiki/Encyclopedia_of_Life_Support_Systems "Encyclopedia of Life Support Systems") under the branch of [technical geography](/wiki/Technical_geography "Technical geography"), which is geared towards interpreting and communicating spatial data.[[3]](#cite_note-Haidu12-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Sala12-4) In Germany, "geodesy and geoinformatics" or "geodesy and geoinformation" is commonly used for describing this discipline. In addition, geospatial engineering is an alternative term to geomatic(s) engineering.

A surveyor's shed showing equipment used for geomatics

**Geomatics** is defined in the [ISO/TC 211](/wiki/ISO/TC_211 "ISO/TC 211") series of standards as the "[discipline](/wiki/Academic_discipline "Academic discipline") concerned with the collection, distribution, storage, analysis, processing, presentation of [geographic data or geographic information](/wiki/Geographic_data_or_geographic_information "Geographic data or geographic information")".[[1]](#cite_note-1) Under another definition, it consists of products, services and tools involved in the collection, integration and management of geographic (geospatial) data.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Surveying engineering was the common name used for geomatics engineering in the past. The term was placed by the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [Encyclopedia of Life Support Systems](/wiki/Encyclopedia_of_Life_Support_Systems "Encyclopedia of Life Support Systems") under the branch of [technical geography](/wiki/Technical_geography "Technical geography"), which is geared towards interpreting and communicating spatial data.[[3]](#cite_note-Haidu12-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Sala12-4) In Germany, "geodesy and geoinformatics" or "geodesy and geoinformation" is commonly used for describing this discipline. In addition, geospatial engineering is an alternative term to geomatic(s) engineering.

## History and etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geomatics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History and etymology")]

The term was proposed in French ("géomatique") at the end of the 1960s by scientist Bernard Dubuisson to reflect at the time recent changes in the jobs of [surveyor](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") and [photogrammetrist](/wiki/Photogrammetry "Photogrammetry").[[5]](#cite_note-5) On June 1, 1971, 'geomatics' was first employed in a [French Ministry of Public Works](/wiki/Minister_of_Public_Works_(France) "Minister of Public Works (France)") memorandum instituting a "standing committee of geomatics" in the government.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

At the centennial congress of the Canadian Institute of Surveying (now known as the [Canadian Institute of Geomatics](/wiki/Canadian_Institute_of_Geomatics "Canadian Institute of Geomatics")) in April 1982, the new classification was further popularised in English by French-Canadian surveyor Michel Paradis in keynote address.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Paradis claimed that at the end of the 20th century the needs for geographical information would reach a scope without precedent in history and that, in order to address these needs, it was necessary to integrate in a new discipline both the traditional disciplines of land surveying and the new tools and techniques of data capture, manipulation, storage and diffusion.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Evolving from its Canadian origins, the term has since been adopted by recognized governmental groups, like the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") and the [Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors](/wiki/Royal_Institution_of_Chartered_Surveyors "Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) Many other international authorities, such as those in the United States, have shown a preference for the term *geospatial technology*, which may be defined as a synonym of "geospatial [information and communications technology](/wiki/Information_and_communications_technology "Information and communications technology")".

## Types of geomatics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geomatics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Types of geomatics")]

Geomatics is an umbrella term that includes the tools and techniques used to analyze the Earth's surface. These can range from [land surveying](/wiki/Land_surveying "Land surveying"), [remote sensing](/wiki/Remote_sensing "Remote sensing"), nautical charts, [geographic information systems](/wiki/Geographic_information_system "Geographic information system") (GIS), and several other related forms of earth [mapping](/wiki/Map "Map"). Some scientists and researchers intend to restrict geomatics to the perspective of [surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") and [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") toward geographic information in order to avoid forming a vague concept.[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Geoinformatics](/wiki/Geoinformatics "Geoinformatics") and [Geographic information science](/wiki/Geographic_information_science "Geographic information science") has been proposed as alternative comprehensive term; however, their popularity is, like geomatics, largely dependent on country.

### Land surveying

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geomatics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Land surveying")]

The methodology of [land surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") includes the measurement and analysis of points on the ground. These readings relay information regarding the angles, distances, and heights, of the points.[[13]](#cite_note-13) It is often regarded as the art and science that helped established land boundaries that cultivated into current, legal property.[[14]](#cite_note-:1-14)

Land surveying is heavily involved with subdivision planning and design, [civil engineering](/wiki/Civil_engineering "Civil engineering"), and construction.[[14]](#cite_note-:1-14)

### Geovisualization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geomatics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Geovisualization")]

[Geovisualization](/wiki/Geovisualization "Geovisualization") combines both [cartography](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography") and [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science") to bring [spatial data](/wiki/Spatial_data "Spatial data") to life. The interactive tools and techniques used assist in supporting exploration and communicate a finished conclusion. As such, the process of knowledge construction is emphasized, unlike traditional maps.[[15]](#cite_note-15) These can be presented in the form of [3D models](/wiki/3D_modeling "3D modeling"), time-lapse animations, and manipulated images.[[16]](#cite_note-:2-16)

The computer processing involved allows users to quickly change visual parameters through filter data layers, which produces an image of higher clarity in relation to static, paper maps.[[17]](#cite_note-17) In relation to geomatics, a geomatics engineer will gather raw data and geovisualization will make this information easily understandable.[[16]](#cite_note-:2-16)

### Hydro geomatics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geomatics&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Hydro geomatics")]

The related field of *hydrogeomatics* covers the area associated with surveying work carried out on, above, or below the surface of the sea or other areas of water.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The subfield is otherwise, and more commonly, known as [hydrography](/wiki/Hydrography "Hydrography"), which was coined in the mid-16th century.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

One pioneer of hydro geomatics is [Alexander Dalrymple](/wiki/Alexander_Dalrymple "Alexander Dalrymple"), the first hydrographer and was appointed by the British navy in 1795. His job was to prep and print charts for travel, thus contributing to naval and merchant shipping.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Dalrymple's history ties directly into the foundational militant ties that the field possesses, and its modern-day scope has widened to include more aspects of hydrogeography from military surveillance to [oceanic habitat conservation](/wiki/Habitat_conservation "Habitat conservation").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) After the UK Hydrographic Office (UKHO) was founded in 1795, the [U.S. Naval Observatory and Hydrographic Office (USNO)](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Observatory "United States Naval Observatory") was officially instituted in 1854, paving the way for safe navigation, global shipping, and defense.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

A U.S. governmental agency called the [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration_Fisheries_Office_of_Law_Enforcement "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Fisheries Office of Law Enforcement") is one example of how hydro geomatics/hydrography is applied. Underwater topography (or [bathymetry](/wiki/Bathymetry "Bathymetry")) is sought after, and common geomatics technology like [multibeam sonars](/wiki/Multibeam_echosounder "Multibeam echosounder") are used to accomplish seabed mapping.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Health geomatics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geomatics&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Health geomatics")]

*Health geomatics* can improve our understanding of the important relationship between location and health, and thus assist us in Public Health tasks like disease prevention, and also in better healthcare service planning.[[25]](#cite_note-25) An important area of research is the use of open data in planning lifesaving activities.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Mining geomatics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geomatics&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mining geomatics")]

*Mining geomatics* is the branch of geomatics dedicated to mining. It focuses on acquiring, processing and analysing spatial data about objects and phenomena in mining environments to support monitoring, modelling, prediction, visualisation and decision-making in mining operations.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Its development is increasingly linked with specialized education and the formation of professional competences adapted to the needs of modern mining.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

A growing number of university departments which were once titled "surveying", "survey engineering" or "[topographic](/wiki/Topographic "Topographic") science" have re-titled themselves using the terms "geomatics" or "geomatics engineering", while others have switched to program titles such as "spatial information technology", and similar names.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The rapid progress and increased visibility of geomatics since the 1990s has been made possible by advances in [computer](/wiki/Computer "Computer") hardware, [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science"), and [software engineering](/wiki/Software_engineering "Software engineering"), as well as by airborne and space observation [remote-sensing](/wiki/Remote-sensing "Remote-sensing") technologies.

### Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geomatics&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS)")]

[![Global Satellite Navigation System (GNSS) orbits and comparison](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Comparison_satellite_navigation_orbits.svg/250px-Comparison_satellite_navigation_orbits.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Comparison_satellite_navigation_orbits.svg)

Global Satellite Navigation System (GNSS) orbits and comparison

[Global navigation satellite systems](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") are a collection of geospatial systems that provide global coverage. The technology has a variety of purposes from communications to mobile navigation.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) The six GNSS constellations in operation are the U.S. [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") Operational Constellation, GLObal NAvigation Satellite System ([GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS")) stemming from Russia, the European [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)"), China's [BeiDou/Compass](/wiki/BeiDou "BeiDou"), Japan's Quasi-Zenith Satellite System ([QZSS](/wiki/Quasi-Zenith_Satellite_System "Quasi-Zenith Satellite System")), and The Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System ([IRNSS](/wiki/Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System "Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System")).

## Geomatics engineering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geomatics&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geomatics engineering")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/TS_by_The_sea.jpg/250px-TS_by_The_sea.jpg)](/wiki/File:TS_by_The_sea.jpg)

Surveyor using a [total station](/wiki/Total_station "Total station")

**Geomatics engineering** is a rapidly developing engineering discipline which focuses on [spatial information](/wiki/Spatial_information "Spatial information") (i.e. information that has a location).[[33]](#cite_note-33) The location is the primary factor used to integrate a very wide range of data for spatial analysis and visualization. Geomatics engineers design, develop, and operate systems for collecting and analyzing spatial information about the land, the oceans, natural resources, and manmade features.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) Geomatics engineers apply engineering principles to spatial information and implement relational data structures involving measurement sciences, thus using geomatics and acting as spatial information engineers. Geomatics engineers manage local, regional, national and global spatial data infrastructures.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Geomatics engineering also involves aspects of Computer Engineering, Software Engineering and Civil Engineering.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Spatial statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geomatics&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Spatial statistics")]

The work of geomatics engineers includes the analysis of [spatial data and statistics](/wiki/Spatial_analysis "Spatial analysis").[[38]](#cite_note-:02-38) This information models "spatially-indexed dependence structures", which combats the idea of an independent and identically distributed set of data.[[38]](#cite_note-:02-38) It is also known as [geospatial analytics](/wiki/Spatial_analysis "Spatial analysis"), and is the information pertaining to a specific location in geospace. The analysis done by geomatics engineers in this field provides actionable insight in accordance to what is being examined.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Subdivision planning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geomatics&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Subdivision planning")]

Working alongside [civil engineers](/wiki/Civil_engineers "Civil engineers"), geomatics engineers will utilize the GNSS and high precision instruments to determine legal and geographic boundaries of an area.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) The raw data is processed through a Geographic Information System (GIS) database, which will then be used as a source by a team.

To assess the most optimal layout, the proposed design is run through constraint data such as floodplains, wetlands, and steep slopes.[[42]](#cite_note-42) A [Subdivision](/wiki/Subdivision_(land) "Subdivision (land)") Plat is prepared, which is the legally recorded map illustrating boundaries, dimensions, and associated partitions.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geomatics&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Impact")]

Geomatics and the technology associated with it has made several breakthroughs in climate change efforts, [population health](/wiki/Population_health "Population health"), and oceanic activities.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-:0-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) This application is especially evident in the use of [photogrammetry](/wiki/Photogrammetry "Photogrammetry"), where images utilized by geomatics can be turned into 3D models.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Furthermore, data from geospatial techniques are employed for governmental use to ameliorate the issues on [Earth's](/wiki/Earth "Earth") surface.[[45]](#cite_note-:0-45)

### Sustainability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geomatics&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Sustainability")]

The ability to interpret geodata is pushing companies in the industry to achieve net-zero emissions.[[48]](#cite_note-:12-48) Agreements and plans across the globe promote climate neutrality such as the Sustainable Developmental Goals (SDGs) and the various editions of the [United Nations Climate Change](/wiki/United_Nations_Climate_Change_Conference "United Nations Climate Change Conference") conference series.[[48]](#cite_note-:12-48)[[49]](#cite_note-:03-49)

The Earth Archive Initiative, launched by Christopher Fisher, aims to create a digital baseline of Earth and mitigate the climate crisis.[[48]](#cite_note-:12-48)[[50]](#cite_note-50) [LiDAR](/wiki/Lidar "Lidar"), a remote sensing technology, will be used to carry out scans of the planet's landmass, which estimates to about 30% of the Earth's surface area. The LiDAR scans would provide a dataset of present data available and the Earth's future state. Doing so will assist in understanding and combating the climate change crisis with a visual representation.[[51]](#cite_note-51)
